conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have a child or children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child - his name is Alex, and he's 10 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know about Alex.

Now, to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you some questions:

Are you responsible for Alex? This means you live with him and are responsible for his day-to-day care (such as providing food, clothing, and looking after his needs)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex doesn't actually live with me. He lives with his mum. But I do help out - I contribute money towards his upkeep each week for things like clothes and bits he needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. It's important to understand the living arrangements.

Since Alex lives with his mum, can you tell me - does his mum currently claim Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, she does claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's very helpful to know.

Just to clarify - are you looking to claim Child Benefit for Alex yourself, or are there other children you're responsible for that you want to claim for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, it's just Alex I was asking about. I wanted to see if I could claim it since I do contribute to his upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me, I'm afraid you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Here's why:

**Child Benefit can only be paid to one person for each child** - the person who is primarily responsible for the child. Being responsible means:
- Living with the child, AND
- Being responsible for their day-to-day care

Since Alex lives with his mum and she is responsible for his day-to-day care, she is the person who qualifies for Child Benefit, which she is already claiming.

While it's commendable that you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep, this alone doesn't make you eligible for Child Benefit when another person (his mum) is the primary carer and already claiming the benefit.

Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child, and priority goes to the person the child lives with and who provides their day-to-day care.

Let me now record this formal decision.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Is there anything else I can help you with regarding Child Benefit or any other questions you may have?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 10 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 10 years old and therefore under 16, which meets the age requirement. However, the claimant does not live with Alex - Alex lives with his mother. The claimant does not have day-to-day responsibility for Alex's care. Child Benefit requires that the claimant is responsible for the child, which means living with them and being responsible for their day-to-day care. Additionally, Alex's mother is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. Although the claimant contributes financially to Alex's upkeep, this does not satisfy the requirement of being responsible for the child's day-to-day care. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 10), who lives with his mother. The mother is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant contributes financially but does not live with Alex or provide day-to-day care. Since Child Benefit can only be paid to one person per child (the person responsible for day-to-day care), and that person is already claiming, the claimant is not eligible.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 10 years old and therefore under 16, which meets the age requirement. However, the claimant does not live with Alex - Alex lives with his mother. The claimant does not have day-to-day responsibility for Alex's care. Child Benefit requires that the claimant is responsible for the child, which means living with them and being responsible for their day-to-day care. Additionally, Alex's mother is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. Although the claimant contributes financially to Alex's upkeep, this does not satisfy the requirement of being responsible for the child's day-to-day care. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 10), who lives with his mother. The mother is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant contributes financially but does not live with Alex or provide day-to-day care. Since Child Benefit can only be paid to one person per child (the person responsible for day-to-day care), and that person is already claiming, the claimant is not eligible.
